title: Guard Rotation
desc: The Deepmines door. Night shift. Six hours of trying not to think.

steps.text:
Night shift at the Doors of the Deepmines. The UnderWorld's least popular assignment.

Your partner for the shift is an UnderWorlder who's done this before — many times, by the look of the deep lines around his eyes. He carries a flask, a book, and a pair of earplugs he hasn't put in.

"Quiet shift, usually," he says. "The doors don't do anything. They just sit there. The problem is what sitting there does to you." He leans against the wall. "Your brain fills the silence with things. Sounds that aren't there. Movements that aren't there. Patterns in the symbols that aren't there."

He opens his book. "I read. Keeps my brain busy. What do you do?"

options.label: Count the symbols on the door frame
Talk — keep the silence at bay

steps.text:
You count. The symbols on the door frame are small, precise, and recursive — each containing smaller versions of itself, like mirrors facing mirrors. You lose count at 300 and start over.

Around the fourth hour, you notice something. One of the symbols near the bottom of the left door is different from the rest. Not in design — in depth. It's cut deeper into the bronze. As if someone traced it over and over.

You crouch down to look. The deep-cut symbol is warm. Not door-warm — the entire door is cold. Just this symbol. Warm, like something is pressing against the other side.

Your partner looks up from his book. "Found the warm one?"

You nod.

"Everyone finds it eventually." He goes back to reading. "It's been warm for as long as anyone's been stationed here. Nobody knows why. Nobody asks anymore."

options.label: Stop counting and just stand guard

steps.text:
You talk. He talks back. You learn that he's been doing this shift for three years. That he requested it. That most guards rotate out after a few months, but he stays.

"Why?"

"Because someone should know the doors. Really know them. Not just stand in front of them — understand them." He taps his book — it's not a novel, it's a notebook. Pages of observations, dates, measurements. The temperature of different sections of the door. The ambient sound levels at different times. The barometric pressure changes that coincide with nothing.

"The doors breathe," he says. "Not literally. But the air pressure on this side fluctuates in patterns. Twelve-hour cycles. Like tides."

Tides. In a sealed underground chamber. Behind doors that nothing passes through.

"I write it down," he says. "Maybe someone, someday, will need to know." He closes the notebook and goes back to watching. The doors don't move. The tides continue.

options.label: Finish the shift in thoughtful silence
